title: trees
content:
📘 Binary Trees – Part 1 (Introduction & Basics)

🔹 1. Introduction

  • Yeh lecture Binary Trees ka introduction hai
  • Pehle tumne yeh data structures dekhe honge:
    • Arrays
    • Stacks
    • Queues
    • Linked List

👉 Yeh sab Linear Data Structures hote hain

🔹 2. Linear vs Hierarchical

✔ Linear Data Structure

  • Data ek line me hota hai
  • Example: Array → ek ke baad ek

✔ Hierarchical Data Structure

  • Data tree structure me hota hai (parent-child relation)
  • Example:
    • Computer folders 📁
      • Ek folder ke andar dusra folder
      • Uske andar aur folders

👉 Isi type ka structure Tree kehlata hai

🔹 3. What is a Binary Tree?

  • "Binary" ka matlab hota hai → 0 ya 1 / ya max 2
  • Binary Tree me:
    • Har node ke maximum 2 children ho sakte hain

👉 Example samajh lo:

  • Ek node ke:
    • 0 child ho sakte hain
    • 1 child ho sakta hai
    • ya max 2 children

❌ 3 children allowed nahi hain

🔹 4. Why Binary Trees Important?

  • Interviews me bahut common topic hai
  • DSA ka major part hai
  • Problems frequently poochi jaati hain

👉 Isliye pura series banaya gaya hai

🔹 5. Basic Terminologies

🔹 Quick Recap

  • Root → starting node
  • Children → niche wale nodes
  • Leaf → jinke children nahi
  • Subtree → node + uske niche sab
  • Ancestors → upar wale sab nodes

Binary Trees – Part 2 (Types of Binary Trees)

🔹 1. Types of Binary Trees

Total 5 types discuss kiye gaye:

  1. Full Binary Tree
  2. Complete Binary Tree
  3. Perfect Binary Tree
  4. Balanced Binary Tree
  5. Degenerate (Skewed) Tree

🔸 2. Full Binary Tree

✔ Definition:

  • Har node ke:
    • ya toh 0 children honge
    • ya exactly 2 children honge

👉 Simple:

  • 1 child allowed nahi hai ❌

✔ Valid Example:

  • Node → 2 children ✔
  • Node → 0 children ✔

❌ Invalid Example:

  • Agar kisi node ka sirf 1 child hai
    👉 Toh wo Full Binary Tree nahi hai

🔹 Shortcut:

👉 “0 ya 2 children only”

🔸 3. Complete Binary Tree

✔ Definition:

  • Sab levels completely filled hone chahiye
  • Except last level
  • Last level me:
    • Nodes left side me hone chahiye

✔ Valid Cases:

Case 1:

  • Har level full ✔

Case 2:

  • Last level incomplete hai
  • But nodes left side me hain ✔


same depth ho mtlb yeh c pe ruk jaata dikkt hojati h and jo jo c ka h woh left me hi ho 

❌ Invalid Case:

  • Last level me gap hai aur node right side me hai

👉 Example:

  • Left empty, right filled ❌

🔹 Shortcut:

👉 “Last level tak left se fill hona chahiye”

🔸 4. Perfect Binary Tree

A binary tree of height 'h' having the maximum number of nodes is a perfect binary tree. 
For a given height h, the maximum number of nodes is 2h+1-1.

✔ Definition:

  • Sab leaf nodes same level pe hone chahiye

✔ Valid:

  • Har leaf same depth pe hai ✔

❌ Invalid:

  • Leaf nodes different levels pe hain ❌

🔹 Shortcut:

👉 “All leaves at same level”

🔸 5. Balanced Binary Tree

✔ Definition:

  • Tree ki height ≈ log(n) honi chahiye
    (n = number of nodes)

✔ Meaning:

  • Tree zyada lamba (skewed) nahi hona chahiye
  • Balanced hona chahiye for efficiency

✔ Example:

  • n = 8
  • log₂(8) ≈ 3
    👉 Height ≈ 3 honi chahiye

❌ Invalid:

  • Agar tree straight line ban gaya
    👉 Height bahut badh jayegi

🔹 Why Important?

  • Fast searching ke liye
  • Time complexity improve hoti hai

🔸 6. Degenerate (Skewed) Tree

✔ Definition:

  • Har node ka sirf 1 child hota hai

✔ Structure:

  • Tree ek linked list jaisa ban jata hai

✔ Types:

  1. Left Skewed
  2. Right Skewed

❌ Problem:

  • Height = n
    👉 Very inefficient

🔹 Shortcut:

👉 “Tree → Linked List jaisa”

🔹 Final Quick Summary

TypeRule
Full0 ya 2 children
CompleteLast level left se fill
PerfectAll leaves same level
BalancedHeight ≈ log(n)
DegenerateHar node ka 1 child

📘 Binary Trees – Representation in C++ (Hinglish Notes)

🔹 1. Basic Idea

  • Binary Tree ko C++ me represent karne ke liye:
    👉 Nodes + Pointers use hote hain
  • Har node ke paas hota hai:
    • Data (value)
    • Left pointer
    • Right pointer

🔸 2. Node Structure Concept

👉 Har node kuch aisa hota hai:

  • Ek value store karta hai
  • Left child ko point karta hai
  • Right child ko point karta hai

✔ Example Tree:

        5
       / \
      6   7
     / \  /
    8   9 10

✔ Pointer Understanding:

  • Node 5:
    • left → 6
    • right → 7
  • Node 6:
    • left → 8
    • right → 9
  • Node 7:
    • left → 10
    • right → NULL
  • Leaf nodes (8, 9, 10):
    • left → NULL
    • right → NULL


🔹 3. Important Rule

👉 Agar kisi node ka child nahi hai:

  • Uska pointer → NULL hota hai

🔸 4. C++ me Node ka Structure

struct Node {
    int data;  // bich wala
    Node* left; // left wala 
    Node* right;  // right wala 

    Node(int val) {
        data = val;
        left = NULL;
        right = NULL;  //  data = val krdenge mtlb koi bhi val and right left ko null kardenge 
    }
};

✔ Explanation:

  • data → value store karega
  • left → left child ka address
  • right → right child ka address

👉 Constructor:

  • Jab node create hota hai:
    • data assign hota hai
    • left & right = NULL

🔹 5. Tree Creation in Main Function

✔ Step-by-step:

Node* root = new Node(1);

👉 Root node create ho gaya (value = 1)

root->left = new Node(2);
root->right = new Node(3);

👉 Ab tree:

    1
   / \
  2   3

✔ Bigger Tree Example:

root->left->left = new Node(4);
root->left->right = new Node(5);

👉 Final tree:

        1
       / \
      2   3
     / \
    4   5

🔹 6. Key Understanding

  • -> ka matlab:
    👉 pointer ke through access
  • new Node(x):
    👉 new node create karta hai

🔹 7. Important Observations

  • Tree manually banaya ja sakta hai
  • Har node dynamically create hota hai
  • Structure flexible hota hai

🔹 8. Quick Summary

  • Binary Tree = Nodes + Pointers
  • Har node:
    • data + left + right
  • Leaf nodes → NULL pointers
  • C++ me:
    • struct Node use hota hai
  • Tree create:
    • new Node() se

📘 Binary Trees – Traversals (DFS & BFS) – Hinglish Notes

🔹 1. Why Traversal Important?

  • Tree me problems solve karne ke liye:
    👉 Tumhe tree traverse (visit) karna padega
  • Without traversal:
    ❌ Tum nodes access hi nahi kar paoge

🔹 2. Types of Traversal

2 main types:

  1. Depth First Search (DFS)
  2. Breadth First Search (BFS)

🌳 DEPTH FIRST SEARCH (DFS)

👉 Depth me jaate hain (deep → subtree → phir back)

🔸 DFS ke 3 Types

  1. Inorder
  2. Preorder
  3. Postorder

🔹 3. Inorder Traversal (Left → Root → Right)

✔ Rule:

👉 Left → Root → Right

✔ Example Tree:

        1
      /   \
     2     3
    / \   / \
   4   5 6   7

✔ Steps:

  • Left subtree → 4 2 5 (lefttest side cover kro )
  • Root → 1 ( root pe aao)
  • Right subtree → 6 3 7 (rightest side pe aao) 

✔ Final Answer:

👉 4 2 5 1 6 3 7

🔹 Trick:

👉 Root beech me aata hai

🔹 4. Preorder Traversal (Root → Left → Right)

✔ Rule:

👉 Root → Left → Right

✔ Steps:

  • Root → 1
  • Left → 2 4 5
  • Right → 3 6 7

✔ Final Answer:

👉 1 2 4 5 3 6 7

🔹 Trick:

👉 Root sabse pehle

🔹 5. Postorder Traversal (Left → Right → Root)

✔ Rule:

👉 Left → Right → Root

✔ Steps:

  • Left → 4 5 2
  • Right → 6 7 3
  • Root → 1

✔ Final Answer:

👉 4 5 2 6 7 3 1

🔹 Trick:

👉 Root sabse last

🔹 6. Easy Memory Trick 🔥

TraversalPatternRoot Position
PreorderRoot Left RightStart
InorderLeft Root RightMiddle
PostorderLeft Right RootEnd

🔹 7. Important Concept

👉 Har subtree pe same logic repeat hota hai (Recursion)

  • Subtree = mini tree
  • Same rule apply karo

🔹 8. Practice Tip (Very Important)

👉 Confusion ho raha hai?

  • 10 trees draw karo ✍️
  • Har ek ka:
    • Inorder
    • Preorder
    • Postorder

👉 100% clear ho jayega

🌳 BREADTH FIRST SEARCH (BFS)

🔹 9. BFS (Level Order Traversal)

✔ Concept:

👉 Tree ko level by level traverse karte hain

✔ Same Example:

        1
      /   \
     2     3
    / \   / \
   4   5 6   7

✔ Levels:

  • Level 0 → 1
  • Level 1 → 2 3
  • Level 2 → 4 5 6 7

✔ BFS Order:

👉 1 2 3 4 5 6 7

🔹 10. DFS vs BFS

FeatureDFSBFS
DirectionDepth (andar tak)Level-wise
Types3 (In, Pre, Post)1
LogicRecursion basedQueue based
DifficultyThoda trickyEasy

🔹 11. Core Difference

  • DFS → Deep jao, phir back aao
  • BFS → Level by level chalo

🔹 Final Quick Summary

  • DFS:
    • Inorder → L R R
    • Preorder → R L R
    • Postorder → L R R
  • BFS:
    • Level order traversal
  • Trees solve karne ke liye:
    👉 Traversal must hai 💯

✅ Ab tumhe:

  • DFS ke 3 types
  • BFS level order
  • Aur unka difference

👉 sab clear hai

📘 Binary Trees – Preorder Traversal (Code + Dry Run)

🔹 1. Preorder Traversal Recap

✔ Rule:

👉 Root → Left → Right

🔹 2. Coding Approach (C++ Logic)

✔ Basic Idea:

  • Agar node NULL hai → return
  • Warna:
    1. Print node
    2. Left subtree pe jao
    3. Right subtree pe jao

✔ Code:

void preorder(Node* node) {
    if (node == NULL) return;

    cout << node->data << " ";   // Root
    preorder(node->left);        // Left
    preorder(node->right);       // Right
}

🔹 3. Important Concept

👉 Yeh recursive solution hai

  • Har node pe same function call hota hai
  • Subtree = chhota tree → same logic apply

🔹 4. Dry Run (Step-by-Step)

✔ Example Tree:

        1
      /   \
     2     3
    / \   / \
   4   5 7   8
          / \
         9  10

✔ Execution Flow:

Step 1:

  • Start at root → 1 print

Step 2:

  • Left jao → 2 print

Step 3:

  • Left jao → 4 print
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return

👉 4 complete ✔

Step 4:

  • Back to 2 → Right jao → 5 print

Step 5:

  • Left jao → 6 print
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return

👉 6 complete ✔

👉 5 complete ✔
👉 2 complete ✔

Step 6:

  • Back to 1 → Right jao → 3 print

Step 7:

  • Left jao → 7 print
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return

👉 7 complete ✔

Step 8:

  • Right jao → 8 print

Step 9:

  • Left jao → 9 print
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return

👉 9 complete ✔

Step 10:

  • Right jao → 10 print
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return

👉 10 complete ✔

👉 8 complete ✔
👉 3 complete ✔
👉 1 complete ✔

🔹 5. Final Preorder Output

👉 1 2 4 5 6 3 7 8 9 10

🔹 6. Key Understanding

  • Pehle print → phir left → phir right
  • Har node ek baar visit hota hai
  • Recursion stack automatically handle karta hai backtracking

🔹 7. Time & Space Complexity

✔ Time Complexity:

👉 O(n)

  • Har node ek baar visit

✔ Space Complexity:

👉 O(h) (height of tree)

  • Worst case (skewed tree):
    👉 O(n)

🔹 8. Super Simple Thinking Trick 🔥

👉 “Jo order chahiye, wahi likho”

  • Root pehle chahiye → print first
  • Left chahiye → left call
  • Right chahiye → right call

👉 Bas 3 lines me solution 💯

🔹 9. Common Mistake

❌ Order galat likh dena
❌ NULL check bhool jana

🔹 Final Summary

  • Preorder = Root Left Right
  • Recursive code = 3 lines
  • Time = O(n)
  • Space = O(h)
  • Dry run practice = must

📘 Binary Trees – Inorder Traversal (Code + Dry Run)


🔹 1. Inorder Traversal Recap

✔ Rule:

👉 Left → Root → Right

🔹 2. Why Recursion Used?

  • Tree me:
    👉 Baar-baar subtree me jaana padta hai
  • Pattern:
    👉 Go → Come back → Go again
  • actually comeback toh hota h i nhi h woh bas har raaste ke liye nya worker bnata h or woh aage ka rasta cover krta h and jese hi null aata h ruk jaat ah 
  • its like jese hi aaye aapko ny dost se khna h aage ki trf jaao its look like backtrack but aise nhi hota 
or jo traversal ka effect aata h kyuki jo phle horha hota h voh phle hi rhta h aagle worker se isleiye woh inoreder or postorder ka effect bhi dekhne ko milta h 

👉 Ye behavior recursion perfectly handle karta hai

🔹 3. Coding Approach (C++ Logic)

✔ Basic Idea:

  • Agar node NULL hai → return
  • Warna:
    1. Left subtree pe jao
    2. Node print karo
    3. Right subtree pe jao

✔ Code:

void inorder(Node* node) {
    if (node == NULL) return;

    inorder(node->left);      // Left
    cout << node->data << " "; // Root
    inorder(node->right);     // Right
}

🔹 4. Dry Run (Step-by-Step)

✔ Example Tree:

        1
      /   \
     2     3
    / \   / \
   4   5 7   8
      /     / \
     6     9  10

🔸 Execution Flow (Important 🔥)

👉 Always follow:
Left → Print → Right

Step 1:

  • Start at root → 1
  • Left jao → 2

Step 2:

  • Left jao → 4
  • Left NULL → return
  • Print → 4
  • Right NULL → return

👉 4 done ✔

Step 3:

  • Back to 2
  • Print → 2

Step 4:

  • Right jao → 5

Step 5:

  • Left jao → 6
  • Left NULL → return
  • Print → 6
  • Right NULL → return

👉 6 done ✔

Step 6:

  • Back to 5
  • Print → 5
  • Right NULL → return

👉 5 done ✔
👉 2 done ✔

Step 7:

  • Back to 1
  • Print → 1

Step 8:

  • Right jao → 3

Step 9:

  • Left jao → 7
  • Left NULL → return
  • Print → 7
  • Right NULL → return

👉 7 done ✔

Step 10:

  • Back to 3
  • Print → 3

Step 11:

  • Right jao → 8

Step 12:

  • Left jao → 9
  • Left NULL → return
  • Print → 9
  • Right NULL → return

👉 9 done ✔

Step 13:

  • Back to 8
  • Print → 8

Step 14:

  • Right jao → 10
  • Left NULL → return
  • Print → 10
  • Right NULL → return

👉 10 done ✔

🔹 5. Final Inorder Output

👉 4 2 6 5 1 7 3 9 8 10

🔹 6. Key Understanding

  • Pehle pura left explore hota hai
  • Fir node print hota hai
  • Fir right explore hota hai

👉 Root hamesha beech me aata hai

🔹 7. Important Observation

👉 Jab NULL milta hai:

  • Function return karta hai
  • Backtracking hoti hai

👉 Isi se recursion flow control hota hai

🔹 8. Time & Space Complexity

✔ Time Complexity:

👉 O(n)

  • Har node ek baar visit

✔ Space Complexity:

👉 O(h) (height)

  • Worst case (skew tree):
    👉 O(n)

🔹 9. Easy Thinking Trick 🔥

👉 “Jo order hai wahi likho”

  • Left chahiye → pehle call
  • Root chahiye → beech me print
  • Right chahiye → last me call

📘 Binary Trees – Postorder Traversal (Code + Dry Run)


🔹 1. Postorder Traversal Recap

✔ Rule:

👉 Left → Right → Root

🔹 Memory Trick 🔥

  • Pre → Root first
  • In → Root middle
  • Post → Root last

👉 “Post = baad me (end me)”

🔹 2. Coding Approach (C++ Logic)

✔ Basic Idea:

  • Agar node NULL hai → return
  • Warna:
    1. Left subtree pe jao
    2. Right subtree pe jao
    3. Node print karo

✔ Code:

void postorder(Node* node) {
    if (node == NULL) return;

    postorder(node->left);     // Left
    postorder(node->right);    // Right
    cout << node->data << " "; // Root
}

🔹 3. Important Concept

👉 Print sabse last me hota hai

  • Pehle left complete
  • Fir right complete
  • Fir root print

🔹 4. Dry Run (Step-by-Step)

✔ Example Tree:

        1
      /   \
     2     3
    / \   / \
   4   5 7   8
      /     / \
     6     9  10

🔸 Execution Flow (Important 🔥)

👉 Always follow:
Left → Right → Print

Step 1:

  • Start at root → 1
  • Left jao → 2

Step 2:

  • Left jao → 4
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return
  • Print → 4

👉 4 done ✔

Step 3:

  • Back to 2
  • Right jao → 5

Step 4:

  • Left jao → 6
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return
  • Print → 6

👉 6 done ✔

Step 5:

  • Back to 5
  • Right NULL → return
  • Print → 5

👉 5 done ✔
👉 2 ke left & right done

Step 6:

  • Print → 2

👉 2 done ✔

Step 7:

  • Back to 1
  • Right jao → 3

Step 8:

  • Left jao → 7
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return
  • Print → 7

👉 7 done ✔

Step 9:

  • Back to 3
  • Right jao → 8

Step 10:

  • Left jao → 9
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return
  • Print → 9

👉 9 done ✔

Step 11:

  • Back to 8
  • Right jao → 10
  • Left NULL → return
  • Right NULL → return
  • Print → 10

👉 10 done ✔

Step 12:

  • Back to 8
  • Print → 8

👉 8 done ✔

Step 13:

  • Back to 3
  • Print → 3

👉 3 done ✔

Step 14:

  • Back to 1
  • Print → 1

👉 1 done ✔

🔹 5. Final Postorder Output

👉 4 6 5 2 7 9 10 8 3 1

🔹 6. Key Understanding

  • Root sabse last me aata hai
  • Pehle pura tree explore hota hai
  • Fir printing hoti hai

Binary Trees – Level Order Traversal (BFS) + Code

🔹 1. What is Level Order Traversal?

👉 Isko Breadth First Search (BFS) bhi kehte hain

✔ Concept:

  • Tree ko level by level traverse karte hain
  • Har level ko left → right order me print karte hain

✔ Example Tree:

        1
      /   \
     2     3
    / \   / \
   4   5 6   7

✔ Level-wise Traversal:

  • Level 0 → 1
  • Level 1 → 2 3
  • Level 2 → 4 5 6 7

✔ Final Output:

👉 1 2 3 4 5 6 7

🔹 2. Key Idea

👉 BFS ke liye hum use karte hain:

  • Queue (FIFO)

🔹 3. Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

✔ Step 1:

  • Queue me root daalo

👉 queue = [1]

✔ Step 2:

  • Queue se element nikalo (pop)
  • Uske:
    • Left child add karo
    • Right child add karo

✔ Step 3:

  • Har level ke nodes ko ek list me store karo

✔ Step 4:

  • Repeat until queue empty


🔹 4. Detailed Flow

🔸 Iteration 1:

  • Queue: [1]
  • Output: [1] 1 ke dono bche daale vector ke andar vector me 
  • Add → 2, 3 then 2 or quie me daaldiye 

👉 Queue: [2, 3]

🔸 Iteration 2:

  • Queue: [2, 3]
  • Output: [2, 3] for 2 and 3 unke left and right daalenge in the vector 
  • jb yeh dal rhe honge vector me toh yeh pop hojayenge 
  • Add → 4, 5, 6, 7  fir inke saare bche list me daaleng e

👉 Queue: [4, 5, 6, 7]

🔸 Iteration 3:

  • Queue: [4, 5, 6, 7]
  • Out  put: [4, 5, 6, 7]  now inke left right dalenge 
  • No children

👉 Queue empty ✅

🔹 5. Data Structures Used

  • Queue → traversal ke liye
  • Vector of Vector → level-wise answer store karne ke liye

🔹 6. C++ Code

vector<vector<int>> levelOrder(Node* root) {
    vector<vector<int>> ans;

    if (root == NULL) return ans;

    queue<Node*> q; // ek queue bnayi 
    q.push(root);   // fir us queue me root push kari

    while (!q.empty()) { // jb tk q empty nhi hoajti yeh chelga 
        int size = q.size();  // its q me jitne element h utha ek size me store krlenge
        vector<int> level;    // ek vector bnate h 

        for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { // loop chalyenge jo push krega vector me 
                                        // fir woh vector baadme main vector me push hoga 
            Node* node = q.front();     // jo chij top pe h pdki 
            q.pop();                   // top wali chij ko nikal diya 

            level.push_back(node->data); // apne level naam ke array me push kiya 

            if (node->left) q.push(node->left);  // us node ke dono left right push kiye 
            if (node->right) q.push(node->right);
        }

        ans.push_back(level);  // then at last usko hi push kardiya 
    }

    return ans;
}

🔹 7. Important Concept

👉 size = q.size() kyu use kiya?

  • Yeh batata hai:
    👉 current level me kitne nodes hain

👉 Taaki hum level-by-level process kar saken

🔹 8. Time & Space Complexity

✔ Time Complexity:

👉 O(n)

  • Har node ek baar visit

✔ Space Complexity:

👉 O(n)

  • Queue me max nodes store ho sakte hain

🔹 9. DFS vs BFS Quick Compare

FeatureDFSBFS
DirectionDepthLevel
DS UsedStack/RecursionQueue
Types31
DifficultyMediumEasy

🔹 10. Easy Understanding Trick 🔥

👉 “Queue = Line”

  • Jo pehle aaya → pehle niklega
  • Level by level kaam hota hai

🔹 Final Summary

  • Level Order = BFS
  • Queue use hoti hai
  • Level-wise traversal hota hai
  • Code me:
    • Queue + loop + size trick
  • Time = O(n), Space = O(n)

✅ Ab tum:

  • BFS samajh gaye
  • Code likh sakte ho
  • DFS vs BFS differentiate kar sakte ho

Detailed Notes: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal Algorithms and Implementation

II. What is a Level Order Traversal?

  • Level order traversal involves traversing a binary tree level by level from left to right.
  • Example Binary Tree Traversal:
    • First Level: Node 1.
    • Second Level (Left to Right): Nodes 2, 3.
    • Third Level (Left to Right): Nodes 4, 5, 6, 7.
  • The traversal follows this fashion: it prints the first level, then the next level, and so on.

III. Initial Data Structures Required

  • To print the level order traversal level-wise, the following data structures are required:
    1. Queue Data Structure: This will initially hold the root of the tree (e.g., node 1).
    2. Result Data Structure: A vector of vectors or an ArrayList of ArrayLists to store the traversal results level-wise.

IV. Step-by-Step Algorithm Iteration

  • Iteration 1 (Root Level):

    • Start iterating over the queue. The first node is 1. Take it out.
    • Check if the left of node 1 exists. If it does (node 2), put it into the queue.
    • Check if the right of node 1 exists. If it does (node 3), put it into the queue.
    • Once done for node 1, put it into the level-wise vector/list and store it in the final data structure.

  • Iteration 2 (Second Level):

    • Whatever is lined up in the queue (nodes 2 and 3) dono ko ek sath , just take them out.
    • For node 2: Check the left (4) and the right (5). Insert both into the queue.
    • For node 3: Check the left (6) and the right (7). Insert both into the queue.
    • Once nodes 2 and 3 are processed, put them into a list/vector and add them to the main data structure storing the level-wise traversals.

  • Iteration 3 (Third Level):

    • The next iteration involves nodes 4, 5, 6, and 7.
    • Take these guys out. Check if they have children.
    • Since nodes 4, 5, 6, and 7 do not have any left or right children, nothing is pushed into the queue.
    • Take these four nodes, put them into a vector, and add them to the required answer data structure (4, 5, 6, 7).

  • Completion:

    • The traversal is complete when the queue data structure is empty.
    • The final data structure now stores the complete level order traversal.

V. Code Implementation Highlights (C++ and Java)

  • Empty Tree Check: Initially, check if the root is empty. If it is, return an empty data structure.
  • Queue Initialization: Define the queue and push the root node into it. 
  • Processing the Levels:
    • Take the size of the queue for each iteration. For the first time, size is 1; then it becomes 2; then 4 ;
    • Use a for loop based on this size to traverse all nodes at that specific level. 
    • Pop the node, check for left and right children, and if they exist, push them into the queue.
    • Store the nodes in a temporary "level" vector and then push that vector into the final "answer" data structure after the loop completes for that level.

VI. Complexity Analysis

  • Time Complexity: Big O(N), because you traverse every node (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7) exactly once.
  • Space Complexity: Big O(N). In the worst case, the queue will store all the nodes at a time.
    • Note: The data structure used to store and return the answer is generally not taken into account for the extra space complexity calculation since it is the required output.

Iterative PREORDER STACK TRAVERSAL

II. Preorder Traversal Definition
  • The goal is to perform a preorder traversal.
  • The order for preorder traversal is: Root, Left, Right.
  • Example Traversal Flow: For a given binary tree, you visit the root, then the left subtree, then the right subtree.
III. Data Structure Required
  • To perform this iteratively without recursion, you require a stack data structure.
  • The stack follows the Last In First Out (LIFO) principle.
IV. Step-by-Step Algorithm (The Logic)
  • Initial Step: Take the root of the tree (e.g., node 1) and put it into your stack.
  • The Loop: Repeat the following process as long as the stack is not empty:
    1. Pop and Print: Look at the top of the stack, take that node out, and print it.
    2. Push Children: Now, check for the children of the node you just popped.
      • Push the Right guy first: Take the right child and put it into the stack.
      • Push the Left guy second: Take the left child and put it into the stack.
      • root left right hota h but yah right left krna h kyuki hum stack ke top se hi khelenge ; 
  • Why Right before Left? Because a stack is LIFO, the node you push last (the left child) will be the first one to come out in the next iteration. This ensures the "Root, Left, Right" order is maintained.
V. Walkthrough Example
  • Tree Root: Node 1.
  • Iteration 1:
    • Stack has .
    • Pop 1, print it.
    • Node 1 has children 2 (left) and 3 (right).
    • Push 3 (right), then push 2 (left).
    • Stack is now .
  • Iteration 2:
    • The top is 2. Pop 2, print it.
    • Check children of 2. If it has children 4 and 5, push 5 (right) then push 4 (left).
    • Stack is now .
  • Iteration 3:
    • The top is 4. Pop 4, print it. (No children to push).
  • Continuing the Process: This continues until the stack is empty.
VI. Code Implementation Highlights
  • Check if the root is null. If it is, return an empty list.
  • Define the stack and push the root initially.
  • While the stack is not empty, pop the top node and add it to your result list.
  • If node->right exists, push it. If node->left exists, push it.
VII. Complexity Analysis
  • Time Complexity: Big O(N). You are traversing every node of the tree exactly once.
  • Space Complexity: Big O(H) (where H is the height of the tree) or Big O(N) in the worst case (for a skewed tree).
    • The stack will store nodes roughly equal to the height of the tree.
    • The data structure used to return the answer is generally not included in the extra space calculation.

Detailed Notes: Iterative Inorder Traversal of a Binary Tree

II. What is Inorder Traversal?

  • Inorder traversal follows the pattern: Left, Node (Root), Right.
  • Example Binary Tree Traversal:
    • Starting at the root, you go left until you hit the first node, which is 4.
    • Then you go to its root, 2.
    • Then you go to the right subtree (6, 5, 7), which also follows left-root-right: 6, 5, 7.
    • Now the entire left portion is complete, so you go to the root: 1.
    • Finally, you go to the right: 3.
    • The full sequence: 4, 2, 6, 5, 7, 1, 3.

III. Moving from Recursion to Iterative

  • In recursion, the nodes are stored in the auxiliary stack space of recursion.
  • To convert this to an iterative solution, you can use a stack data structure created by yourself.
  • A stack is a Last In First Out (LIFO) data structure.
  • You also need a node initialized to the root initially.

IV. Step-by-Step Algorithm (The Logic)

  • Iteration 1 (Moving Left):

    • Currently, you are at node 1. If the node is not null, take it and put it into your stack.
    • Now, move to the left of 1, which is 2.
    • In the next iteration, node is 2 (not null). Store it in the stack and move to the left, which is 4.
    • In the next iteration, node is 4. Store it and move to the left, which is null.
    • jb tk left mil rha h jaate rho or stack me daalte rho

  • Iteration 2 (Processing Null and Backtracking):

    • When you encounter a null, there is no need to move left further.
    • left null arha h toh right check kro woh bhi null toh stack ka top pop kro print kro 
    • Similar to recursion, you take out the element from the stack and print it. The first element printed is 4.
    • After printing, you must move to the right. Node 4's right is null, so the node beco mes null again.
    • phle null se 4 pop hua or print hua dusre se 2 pop hua or print hua 

  • Iteration 3 (Printing Root and Moving to Right Subtree):

    • Coming back to the next iteration with a null, you take the next top of the stack, which is 2, and print it.
    • Since you've printed 2 (Left-Root is done), it's time to move to- 2's right, which is 5.

  • Iteration 4 (Processing Node 5 and 6):

    • Now at node 5, put it into the stack and move to its left, which is 6.
    • At node 6, put it into the stack and move to its left, which is null.
    • Since it's null, take the top of the stack (6) and print it.
    • Now move to 6's right, which is null.

  • Iteration 5 (Backtracking to 5 and Node 7):

    • Encounters another null, so take the next guy from the stack, 5, and print it.
    • Now move to 5's right, which is 7.
    • At node 7, put it into the stack and move to its left, which is null.
    • Encounters null, take 7 out of the stack and print it.
    • Move to 7's right, which is null.

  • Iteration 6 (Completing the Tree):

    • Encounters null, take the top element 1 and print it.
    • Move to the right of 1, which is 3.
    • At node 3, put it into the stack and move to its left, which is null.
    • Encounters null, take 3 out and print it.
    • Move to 3's right, which is null.
    • The next iteration sees a null and the stack is empty. You can now break out.

V. Code Implementation Highlights (C++ and Java)

  • Create an empty stack and a node assigned to root.
  • Use a vector/list to store the inorder traversal.
  • The Loop:
    • If node is not null: Push the node to the stack and move to the left.
    • Else (node is null): If the stack is empty, it's over. Otherwise, take the top of the stack, add it to your inorder list, and move to its right.

VI. Complexity Analysis

  • Time Complexity: Big O(N), because you are traversing all the nodes once.
  • Space Complexity: Big O(H) (height of the tree) or Big O(N) in the worst case (for a skewed tree where every node only has a left child).
    • The answer data structure is generally not counted as extra space.

node* root = new node(1);
    root->left = new node(2);
    node* two = root->left ;
    two->left = new node(3);
    two->right = new node(4);
    two->right->left = new node(5);
    two->right->right = new node(6);
    root->right = new node(7);
   
    node* curr = root ;
    vector<int> inorder;
    stack<node*> st ;
   
    while(true){
      if(curr!=NULL){
        st.push(curr);
        curr=curr->left;
      }
      else{
        if(st.empty()==true) break ;
        curr=st.top();
        inorder.push_back(curr->data);
        st.pop();
        curr = curr->right ;
      }

    }

for(int it : inorder)
{
cout << it ;
}
}
